quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Faraday's law"?

options.text: ε = -dΦ_B/dt; induced EMF equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux; the basis for generators and transformers
The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases; natural processes are irreversible
d_o > 0 (real object); d_i > 0 (real image, opposite side); d_i  0 (converging); f < 0 (diverging)
Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Faraday's law" is defined as: ε = -dΦ_B/dt; induced EMF equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux; the basis for generators and transformers. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Lenz's law"?

options.text: Peaks at iron-56; lighter nuclei release energy by fusion; heavier nuclei release energy by fission
ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop; based on conservation of energy; EMF gains = resistive drops
The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the flux change that caused it; a consequence of energy conservation
Bright colored lines on a dark background; produced by excited atoms emitting photons at characteristic frequencies

quiz_questions.explanation: "Lenz's law" is defined as: The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the flux change that caused it; a consequence of energy conservation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Motional EMF"?

options.text: ε = BLv; induced when a conductor of length L moves with velocity v perpendicular to field B
P_total = P₁ + P₂; equivalent to adding the reciprocals of focal lengths
v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself
Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects

quiz_questions.explanation: "Motional EMF" is defined as: ε = BLv; induced when a conductor of length L moves with velocity v perpendicular to field B. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Eddy currents"?

options.text: Induced currents in bulk conductors; dissipate energy as heat; used in magnetic braking and induction heating
B = μ₀I/(2πr); circular field lines around a long straight wire; strength decreases with distance r from wire
β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) in decibels (dB); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing; each 10 dB = 10× intensity
Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern

quiz_questions.explanation: "Eddy currents" is defined as: Induced currents in bulk conductors; dissipate energy as heat; used in magnetic braking and induction heating. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Self-inductance"?

options.text: Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier
Net E at a point = vector sum of fields from all individual charges; requires component-wise addition
Energy needed to completely remove an electron: for hydrogen, E_ionization = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to n=∞)
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Self-inductance" is defined as: ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ε = -dΦ_B/dt; induced EMF equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux; the basis for generators and transformers"?

options.text: Entropy
Faraday's law
Energy levels
Snell's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Faraday's law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the flux change that caused it; a consequence of energy conservation"?

options.text: Sound level addition
Lenz's law
Dispersion
Doppler effect

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Lenz's law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ε = BLv; induced when a conductor of length L moves with velocity v perpendicular to field B"?

options.text: Motional EMF
Standing waves
Quantum tunneling
Snell's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Motional EMF".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Induced currents in bulk conductors; dissipate energy as heat; used in magnetic braking and induction heating"?

options.text: Speed of sound
Beta-minus decay
Eddy currents
Reversible vs. irreversible

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Eddy currents".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)"?

options.text: Standard Model
Huygens' principle
Self-inductance
Sound intensity level

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Self-inductance".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Electromagnetic Induction is accurate?

options.text: Electromagnetic Induction is described by Faraday's law: a changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an EMF: ε = -dΦ_B/dt.
The dielectric reduces the electric field between plates and allows more charge to be stored at the same voltage.
Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.
An absorption spectrum shows dark lines at the same wavelengths when continuous light passes through a cool gas—the gas absorbs photons matching its transition energies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Induction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Electromagnetic Induction?

options.text: Energy carried by waves is proportional to amplitude squared: E ∝ A².
Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave produced by vibrating objects.
Each element has a unique spectrum.
Lenz's law states the induced current opposes the change in flux (the negative sign).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Induction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Electromagnetic Induction?

options.text: This force is short-range (~1 fm), extremely strong, and charge-independent.
The force on a current loop creates torque: τ = nIAB sin θ, the basis for electric motors.
EMF can be induced by changing B, changing A, or changing the angle θ between B and the area vector.
In hydrogen: E_n = -13.6 eV/n².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Induction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Electromagnetic Induction?

options.text: The force on a current loop creates torque: τ = nIAB sin θ, the basis for electric motors.
Optical Instruments combine lenses and mirrors to extend human vision.
For a coil of N turns: ε = -N·dΦ_B/dt.
Magnetism studies magnetic fields and the forces they exert on moving charges and current-carrying conductors.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Induction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Electromagnetic Induction?

options.text: Motional EMF: when a conductor moves through a field, ε = BLv (B = field, L = conductor length, v = velocity).
Magnetism studies magnetic fields and the forces they exert on moving charges and current-carrying conductors.
Fission is the basis of nuclear reactors; fusion powers the Sun.
Reflection and Mirrors covers how light bounces off surfaces.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Induction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Induction?

options.text: Defibrillators deliver a large potential difference (~1000-3000 V) across the heart to reset its electrical rhythm during cardiac arrest
Night vision devices use the photoelectric effect: photons from dim scenes eject electrons from a photocathode, which are then amplified to produce a visible image
Electric generators convert mechanical energy to electrical energy: rotating coils in magnetic fields produce alternating EMF (AC power) via Faraday's law
Camera lens design: modern camera lenses contain 10-20 elements to correct for aberrations, provide zoom capability, and maintain image sharpness across the frame

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Induction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Electromagnetic Induction?

options.text: Sonar uses sound pulse travel time in water to measure ocean depth and detect objects—essential for submarine navigation and marine biology research
Velocity selectors in mass spectrometers: only ions with the correct speed enter the separation chamber, improving measurement accuracy
The James Webb Space Telescope uses a 6.5m segmented gold-coated mirror to collect infrared light from the earliest galaxies in the universe
Induction cooktops: rapidly alternating magnetic fields induce eddy currents in the metal pot, heating it directly while the cooktop surface stays cool

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Induction.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Electromagnetic Induction applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone
Noise barriers on highways: effective at blocking high-frequency (short λ) sound but less effective for low-frequency (long λ) sound that diffracts over the top
PET-CT combines metabolic imaging (PET using ¹⁸F-FDG) with anatomical imaging (CT X-rays) for precise cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring
Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Induction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Electromagnetic Induction?

options.text: Hall effect sensors are used as position detectors in smartphones, automotive systems (crankshaft position), and brushless DC motor commutation
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts
Rutherford's experiment was done with gold because it's malleable enough to make foils only a few atoms thick—thicker foils would scatter too many alpha particles
Electric generators convert mechanical energy to electrical energy: rotating coils in magnetic fields produce alternating EMF (AC power) via Faraday's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Induction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Electromagnetic Induction in practice?

options.text: Endoscopes use fiber optic bundles and tiny lens systems to allow doctors to view inside the body without surgery—used in colonoscopy, arthroscopy, and laparoscopy
Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length
Induction cooktops: rapidly alternating magnetic fields induce eddy currents in the metal pot, heating it directly while the cooktop surface stays cool
Energy storage: supercapacitors bridge the gap between batteries and conventional capacitors, providing rapid charge/discharge for regenerative braking in electric vehicles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Induction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Diamagnetism"?

options.text: F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)
Weak repulsion from B fields; present in all materials; caused by induced atomic dipole moments opposing the applied field
Uses r = mv/(qB) to separate ions by mass: heavier ions curve less (larger r), lighter ions curve more (smaller r)
F = kq₁q₂/r²; k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²; inversely proportional to r²; analogous to gravitational force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Diamagnetism" is defined as: Weak repulsion from B fields; present in all materials; caused by induced atomic dipole moments opposing the applied field. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Paramagnetism"?

options.text: 1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹
Fission neutrons trigger more fissions; critical mass: self-sustaining; supercritical: explosive; moderated in reactors
Weak attraction to B fields; atoms have permanent magnetic dipoles that partially align with the field; effect disappears when field is removed
ε = BLv; induced when a conductor of length L moves with velocity v perpendicular to field B

quiz_questions.explanation: "Paramagnetism" is defined as: Weak attraction to B fields; atoms have permanent magnetic dipoles that partially align with the field; effect disappears when field is removed. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ferromagnetism"?

options.text: V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)
V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁; ideal transformers conserve power: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂; stepping up voltage steps down current
Strong attraction and permanent magnetization; magnetic domains align cooperatively; responsible for permanent magnets
Zero electrical resistance below a critical temperature T_c; enables powerful electromagnets (MRI: 1.5-7T fields) and lossless power transmission

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ferromagnetism" is defined as: Strong attraction and permanent magnetization; magnetic domains align cooperatively; responsible for permanent magnets. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Magnetic domains"?

options.text: Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions
Microscopic regions within ferromagnets where all atomic dipoles are aligned; applying a field causes domains to grow or rotate
η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H; the maximum possible efficiency for an engine operating between temperatures T_H and T_C (in Kelvin)
Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars

quiz_questions.explanation: "Magnetic domains" is defined as: Microscopic regions within ferromagnets where all atomic dipoles are aligned; applying a field causes domains to grow or rotate. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Curie temperature"?

options.text: The temperature above which a ferromagnet becomes paramagnetic; iron: 770°C; nickel: 358°C; irreversible heating can demagnetize
f_observed = f_source · (v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source); approaching gives higher pitch, receding gives lower pitch
Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern
Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted

quiz_questions.explanation: "Curie temperature" is defined as: The temperature above which a ferromagnet becomes paramagnetic; iron: 770°C; nickel: 358°C; irreversible heating can demagnetize. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Weak repulsion from B fields; present in all materials; caused by induced atomic dipole moments opposing the applied field"?

options.text: E-V relationship
Diamagnetism
Nuclear fusion
Rydberg formula

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Diamagnetism".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Weak attraction to B fields; atoms have permanent magnetic dipoles that partially align with the field; effect disappears when field is removed"?

options.text: Paramagnetism
Electric current
Continuity equation
Malus's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Paramagnetism".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Strong attraction and permanent magnetization; magnetic domains align cooperatively; responsible for permanent magnets"?

options.text: Buoyant force (Archimedes)
Continuity equation
Bernoulli's equation
Ferromagnetism

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ferromagnetism".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Microscopic regions within ferromagnets where all atomic dipoles are aligned; applying a field causes domains to grow or rotate"?

options.text: Photon absorption
Magnetic domains
Standing waves
Emission spectrum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Magnetic domains".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The temperature above which a ferromagnet becomes paramagnetic; iron: 770°C; nickel: 358°C; irreversible heating can demagnetize"?

options.text: Photon emission
Nuclear binding energy
Curie temperature
Right-hand rule (force)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Curie temperature".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Magnetic Properties of Materials is accurate?

options.text: Magnetic Properties of Materials classifies materials by their response to external magnetic fields.
The second law states that natural processes increase the total entropy of the universe; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse, without external work.
Sound intensity follows an inverse-square law: I ∝ 1/r².
The kinetic theory of gases derives macroscopic properties from molecular motion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetic Properties of Materials as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Magnetic Properties of Materials?

options.text: Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).
Diamagnetic materials (copper, bismuth) are weakly repelled by fields—induced dipoles oppose the external field.
Electric Potential relates to the potential energy per unit charge in an electric field.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetic Properties of Materials as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Magnetic Properties of Materials?

options.text: The force on a current loop creates torque: τ = nIAB sin θ, the basis for electric motors.
Paramagnetic materials (aluminum, platinum) are weakly attracted—atomic dipoles partially align with the field.
Each element has a unique spectrum.
Pressure is defined as force per unit area: P = F/A.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetic Properties of Materials as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Magnetic Properties of Materials?

options.text: Ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt) are strongly attracted—magnetic domains align cooperatively, creating permanent magnets.
Total internal reflection occurs when light hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle: θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁), and only when going from high-n to low-n medium.
Fission is the basis of nuclear reactors; fusion powers the Sun.
In hydrogen: E_n = -13.6 eV/n².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetic Properties of Materials as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Magnetic Properties of Materials?

options.text: Sound intensity follows an inverse-square law: I ∝ 1/r².
Curie temperature: above this temperature, ferromagnets lose their permanent magnetism and become paramagnetic.
Energy and Mass Equivalence is summarized by Einstein's famous equation E = mc².
Applications of Quantum Ideas extends quantum principles to technologies and fundamental physics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetic Properties of Materials as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Magnetic Properties of Materials?

options.text: Electric field therapy (tumor treating fields): alternating electric fields disrupt cancer cell division—a novel application of electrostatics in medicine
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
Musical instrument design: the length and shape of brass instruments determine which harmonics are produced; a bugle plays only natural harmonics of its tube length
Hard disk drives store data using ferromagnetic domains: a write head magnetizes tiny regions in specific orientations, representing binary 0s and 1s

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetic Properties of Materials.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Magnetic Properties of Materials?

options.text: MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules
Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing
Phone chargers contain transformers (and rectifiers) that convert 120V AC to 5-20V DC for safe device charging
Charged particle therapy: proton beams for cancer treatment are steered using electromagnetic forces to precisely target tumors deep in the body

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetic Properties of Materials.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Magnetic Properties of Materials applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Quantum computing chips use superconducting circuits (transmon qubits) cooled to 15 millikelvin—colder than outer space—to maintain quantum coherence for computation
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)
The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Magnetic levitation: diamagnetic materials (like pyrolytic graphite) can levitate in strong magnetic fields—demonstrated in physics labs and proposed for frictionless bearings

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetic Properties of Materials.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Magnetic Properties of Materials?

options.text: Hard disk drives store data using ferromagnetic domains: a write head magnetizes tiny regions in specific orientations, representing binary 0s and 1s
Endoscopes use fiber optic bundles and tiny lens systems to allow doctors to view inside the body without surgery—used in colonoscopy, arthroscopy, and laparoscopy
Electric field therapy (tumor treating fields): alternating electric fields disrupt cancer cell division—a novel application of electrostatics in medicine
Airplane wing lift: the curved upper surface forces air to move faster (lower pressure above) than the flat lower surface (higher pressure below), creating net upward force via Bernoulli's principle

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetic Properties of Materials.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Magnetic Properties of Materials in practice?

options.text: Batteries maintain a fixed potential difference (EMF) between terminals; the chemical reaction supplies energy to move charges from low to high potential
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)
Fluorescent lighting: UV photons from mercury vapor excite phosphor coating electrons, which emit visible light as they return to lower energy levels
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetic Properties of Materials.

summary_sections.title: Understanding Alternating Current
summary_sections.content_html: Alternating Current is an important topic in AP Physics 2 covering fundamental concepts and principles.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts and Vocabulary
summary_sections.content_html: Key concepts in Alternating Current include terminology, processes, and relationships specific to this topic.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications and Examples

flashcards.question: Define: Thermodynamics
flashcards.answer: The study of heat, energy, and the relationships between thermal, mechanical, and other forms of energy

flashcards.question: Define: Entropy
flashcards.answer: A measure of disorder in a system; the second law states that entropy in an isolated system always increases

flashcards.question: Define: Electric Field
flashcards.answer: A region of space around a charged object where another charged object experiences a force

flashcards.question: Define: Magnetic Field
flashcards.answer: A region of space around a magnet or moving charge where magnetic forces are exerted

flashcards.question: Define: Electromagnetic Wave
flashcards.answer: A wave that consists of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation

flashcards.question: Define: Capacitor
flashcards.answer: A device that stores electrical energy by accumulating electric charge on two conducting plates

flashcards.question: Define: Resistance
flashcards.answer: The opposition to the flow of electric current through a material, measured in ohms

flashcards.question: Define: Interference
flashcards.answer: The phenomenon where two or more waves overlap and combine to create a resultant wave

flashcards.question: Which of the following is the primary way water returns to the atmosphere?
flashcards.answer: Evaporation from oceans and transpiration from plants

flashcards.question: Which human activity most directly affects the water cycle?
flashcards.answer: Urbanization reduces infiltration and increases runoff

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "AC voltage"?

options.text: Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars
A quantum system exists in multiple states simultaneously until measured; the wave function collapses to one state upon observation
ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop; based on conservation of energy; EMF gains = resistive drops
V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt); alternates between +V₀ and -V₀; ω = 2πf; US standard: 120V RMS at 60 Hz

quiz_questions.explanation: "AC voltage" is defined as: V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt); alternates between +V₀ and -V₀; ω = 2πf; US standard: 120V RMS at 60 Hz. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "RMS values"?

options.text: Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)
The image from lens 1 serves as the object for lens 2; d_o2 = separation - d_i1; apply thin lens equation sequentially
V_rms = V₀/√2 ≈ 0.707V₀; I_rms = I₀/√2; the effective DC-equivalent values for computing average power
ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei

quiz_questions.explanation: "RMS values" is defined as: V_rms = V₀/√2 ≈ 0.707V₀; I_rms = I₀/√2; the effective DC-equivalent values for computing average power. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Average power"?

options.text: θ_incident = θ_reflected; both angles measured from the normal to the surface
P_avg = I_rms · V_rms = ½V₀I₀ for resistive loads; P = I²R uses I_rms for AC circuits
τ = nIAB sin θ; n = number of turns, I = current, A = loop area, B = field strength, θ = angle between area vector and B
F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B

quiz_questions.explanation: "Average power" is defined as: P_avg = I_rms · V_rms = ½V₀I₀ for resistive loads; P = I²R uses I_rms for AC circuits. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Transformer equation"?

options.text: k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K; relates temperature to individual molecular energy
Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions
V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁; ideal transformers conserve power: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂; stepping up voltage steps down current
ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop; based on conservation of energy; EMF gains = resistive drops

quiz_questions.explanation: "Transformer equation" is defined as: V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁; ideal transformers conserve power: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂; stepping up voltage steps down current. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Power transmission"?

options.text: Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT
E = hf = hc/λ; h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant); higher frequency = higher energy per photon
High-voltage transmission minimizes I²R losses: P_loss = I²R = P²R/V²; higher V → lower I → much lower losses
When a driving frequency matches a natural frequency of the tube, standing waves form and the sound is amplified dramatically

quiz_questions.explanation: "Power transmission" is defined as: High-voltage transmission minimizes I²R losses: P_loss = I²R = P²R/V²; higher V → lower I → much lower losses. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt); alternates between +V₀ and -V₀; ω = 2πf; US standard: 120V RMS at 60 Hz"?

options.text: AC voltage
Continuity equation
Mass spectrometer
Magnetic force on a charge

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "AC voltage".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "V_rms = V₀/√2 ≈ 0.707V₀; I_rms = I₀/√2; the effective DC-equivalent values for computing average power"?

options.text: Total internal reflection
Magnetic flux
RMS values
Heisenberg uncertainty principle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "RMS values".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P_avg = I_rms · V_rms = ½V₀I₀ for resistive loads; P = I²R uses I_rms for AC circuits"?

options.text: Kinetic theory
Photon
Average power
Photon emission

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Average power".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁; ideal transformers conserve power: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂; stepping up voltage steps down current"?

options.text: Quantum mechanical model
Dielectric constant (κ)
Transformer equation
De Broglie wavelength

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Transformer equation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "High-voltage transmission minimizes I²R losses: P_loss = I²R = P²R/V²; higher V → lower I → much lower losses"?

options.text: Compound microscope
Power transmission
Standing waves
Brewster's angle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Power transmission".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Alternating Current is accurate?

options.text: Alternating Current (AC) periodically reverses direction.
The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
Power dissipated: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.
The modern quantum model replaces orbits with probability clouds (orbitals) described by wave functions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Alternating Current as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Alternating Current?

options.text: The standing-wave condition (electron wavelength must fit the orbital circumference: nλ = 2πr) explains quantization.
Curie temperature: above this temperature, ferromagnets lose their permanent magnetism and become paramagnetic.
An absorption spectrum shows dark lines at the same wavelengths when continuous light passes through a cool gas—the gas absorbs photons matching its transition energies.
AC voltage is described by V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt), where V₀ is peak voltage and ω = 2πf.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Alternating Current as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Alternating Current?

options.text: The root-mean-square (RMS) values are V_rms = V₀/√2 and I_rms = I₀/√2, which give the equivalent DC values for power calculations: P_avg = I_rms · V_rms.
De Broglie extended wave-particle duality: all matter has a wavelength λ = h/p.
Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.
Like mirrors, the thin lens equation 1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f applies (with sign conventions).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Alternating Current as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Alternating Current?

options.text: An emission spectrum consists of bright lines at specific wavelengths produced when excited gas atoms emit photons during electron transitions downward.
Diamagnetic materials (copper, bismuth) are weakly repelled by fields—induced dipoles oppose the external field.
Transformers use electromagnetic induction to change voltage levels: V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁.
Field lines point away from positive charges and toward negative charges.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Alternating Current as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Alternating Current?

options.text: The speed of sound depends on the medium's elastic and inertial properties: v = √(B/ρ) for fluids and v ≈ 331 + 0.6T m/s in air (T in °C).
Beta-plus decay (β⁺): proton → neutron + positron + neutrino (Z→Z-1).
Step-up transformers increase voltage (for transmission), step-down transformers decrease it (for household use).
Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Alternating Current as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Alternating Current?

options.text: PET-CT combines metabolic imaging (PET using ¹⁸F-FDG) with anatomical imaging (CT X-rays) for precise cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring
CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen
The electrical grid: power plants generate at ~20 kV, transformers step up to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission, then step down to 120/240V for homes—reducing losses by factors of thousands
Radon testing: radon-222 (α emitter, t₁/₂ = 3.8 days) seeps from soil into basements; second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Alternating Current.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Alternating Current?

options.text: Stellar composition: absorption lines in starlight reveal which elements are in the star's atmosphere; helium was first discovered in the Sun's spectrum before being found on Earth
Concert hall acoustics: architects design room dimensions, wall shapes, and absorption materials to optimize reverberation time (1.5-2.5 seconds ideal for orchestras)
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
Phone chargers contain transformers (and rectifiers) that convert 120V AC to 5-20V DC for safe device charging

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Alternating Current.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Alternating Current applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Induction motors: AC current creates rotating magnetic fields that induce currents in the rotor, producing torque—powering most industrial machinery and household appliances
Energy storage: supercapacitors bridge the gap between batteries and conventional capacitors, providing rapid charge/discharge for regenerative braking in electric vehicles
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
Noise barriers on highways: effective at blocking high-frequency (short λ) sound but less effective for low-frequency (long λ) sound that diffracts over the top

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Alternating Current.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Alternating Current?

options.text: Refrigerators work as reverse heat engines, moving heat from cold interior to warm exterior—they require work input, consistent with the second law
Battery internal resistance: real batteries have internal resistance r, so terminal voltage = EMF - Ir; explains why batteries 'die' under heavy load
The electrical grid: power plants generate at ~20 kV, transformers step up to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission, then step down to 120/240V for homes—reducing losses by factors of thousands
Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Alternating Current.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Alternating Current in practice?

options.text: Smoke detectors: americium-241 emits alpha particles that ionize air, creating a current; smoke disrupts the current, triggering the alarm
Defibrillators deliver a large potential difference (~1000-3000 V) across the heart to reset its electrical rhythm during cardiac arrest
Scanning tunneling microscopes use quantum tunneling current between a sharp tip and a surface to image individual atoms on surfaces with sub-atomic resolution
Phone chargers contain transformers (and rectifiers) that convert 120V AC to 5-20V DC for safe device charging

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Alternating Current.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Law of reflection"?

options.text: Reversible: idealized, infinitely slow, maximum work; irreversible: real, finite speed, always produces less work and more entropy
V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁; ideal transformers conserve power: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂; stepping up voltage steps down current
θ_incident = θ_reflected; both angles measured from the normal to the surface
E = hf = hc/λ; h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant); higher frequency = higher energy per photon

quiz_questions.explanation: "Law of reflection" is defined as: θ_incident = θ_reflected; both angles measured from the normal to the surface. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Concave mirror"?

options.text: Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return
Converging mirror: f > 0; produces real images when object is beyond F; virtual images when object is inside F
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Concave mirror" is defined as: Converging mirror: f > 0; produces real images when object is beyond F; virtual images when object is inside F. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Convex mirror"?

options.text: Diverging mirror: f < 0; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images; field of view is wider than flat mirrors
F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B
Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return
Total charge in an isolated system remains constant; charges can transfer between objects but cannot be created or destroyed

quiz_questions.explanation: "Convex mirror" is defined as: Diverging mirror: f < 0; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images; field of view is wider than flat mirrors. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mirror equation"?

options.text: J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron
The image from lens 1 serves as the object for lens 2; d_o2 = separation - d_i1; apply thin lens equation sequentially
Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions
1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; d_o = object distance, d_i = image distance, f = focal length; sign conventions determine image type

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mirror equation" is defined as: 1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; d_o = object distance, d_i = image distance, f = focal length; sign conventions determine image type. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Magnification"?

options.text: Total charge in an isolated system remains constant; charges can transfer between objects but cannot be created or destroyed
Induced currents in bulk conductors; dissipate energy as heat; used in magnetic braking and induction heating
m = -d_i/d_o = h_i/h_o; m > 0: upright image; m  1: enlarged; |m| < 1: reduced
Weak attraction to B fields; atoms have permanent magnetic dipoles that partially align with the field; effect disappears when field is removed

quiz_questions.explanation: "Magnification" is defined as: m = -d_i/d_o = h_i/h_o; m > 0: upright image; m  1: enlarged; |m| < 1: reduced. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "θ_incident = θ_reflected; both angles measured from the normal to the surface"?

options.text: Rutherford model
Law of reflection
Beats
Average power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Law of reflection".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Converging mirror: f > 0; produces real images when object is beyond F; virtual images when object is inside F"?

options.text: Apparent depth
Concave mirror
Wien's displacement law
Malus's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Concave mirror".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Diverging mirror: f < 0; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images; field of view is wider than flat mirrors"?

options.text: Convex mirror
Sound level addition
Right-hand rule (force)
Rydberg formula

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Convex mirror".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; d_o = object distance, d_i = image distance, f = focal length; sign conventions determine image type"?

options.text: Mirror equation
De Broglie wavelength
Electric potential (voltage)
Bernoulli's equation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mirror equation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "m = -d_i/d_o = h_i/h_o; m > 0: upright image; m  1: enlarged; |m| < 1: reduced"?

options.text: Malus's law
Magnification
Bernoulli's equation
Electric potential (voltage)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Magnification".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Reflection and Mirrors is accurate?

options.text: Inserting a dielectric (insulating material) between the plates increases capacitance by a factor κ: C = κε₀A/d.
The modern quantum model replaces orbits with probability clouds (orbitals) described by wave functions.
Electric Circuits involve the flow of charge through conductive paths driven by a potential difference.
Reflection and Mirrors covers how light bounces off surfaces.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reflection and Mirrors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Reflection and Mirrors?

options.text: Total internal reflection occurs when light hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle: θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁), and only when going from high-n to low-n medium.
Planck proposed that energy is emitted/absorbed in discrete quanta: E = nhf.
Heat engines convert thermal energy to work with efficiency η = W/Q_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H.
The law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection (θᵢ = θᵣ), measured from the normal.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reflection and Mirrors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Reflection and Mirrors?

options.text: Mass and energy are interconvertible: the mass defect in nuclear reactions is converted to kinetic energy of products and radiation.
The energy level diagram shows allowed states; arrows between levels represent possible transitions.
Lenses use refraction to form images.
Plane mirrors produce virtual, upright, same-size images located behind the mirror.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reflection and Mirrors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Reflection and Mirrors?

options.text: Rutherford's gold foil experiment proved atoms have tiny, dense, positive nuclei.
Superposition: the net electric field from multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields.

Concave mirrors (converging) can produce real or virtual images depending on object distance: objects beyond the focal point produce inverted real images; objects inside the focal point produce upright virtual images.
The strong nuclear force binds nucleons together, overcoming electrostatic repulsion between protons.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reflection and Mirrors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Reflection and Mirrors?

options.text: Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.
Convex mirrors (diverging) always produce virtual, upright, reduced images.
Superposition: the net electric field from multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields.
The intensity and spectrum of thermal radiation depend on temperature (Wien's law: λ_max = b/T; Stefan-Boltzmann: P = σAT⁴).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reflection and Mirrors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Reflection and Mirrors?

options.text: Flash memory (USB drives, SSDs) stores data using quantum tunneling: electrons tunnel through a thin oxide layer to charge a floating gate, representing binary data
Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots
Hydraulic systems use Pascal's principle: a small force on a small piston creates a large force on a large piston (F₂ = F₁ · A₂/A₁), powering car brakes and industrial presses
Musical instrument design: the length and shape of brass instruments determine which harmonics are produced; a bugle plays only natural harmonics of its tube length

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reflection and Mirrors.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Reflection and Mirrors?

options.text: Telescopes use concave mirrors to collect and focus starlight: the Hubble Space Telescope has a 2.4-meter primary concave mirror
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Airplane wing lift: the curved upper surface forces air to move faster (lower pressure above) than the flat lower surface (higher pressure below), creating net upward force via Bernoulli's principle
Weather balloons expand as they rise because atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, and Boyle's law predicts volume increase at lower pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reflection and Mirrors.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Reflection and Mirrors applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Solar concentrators use parabolic concave mirrors to focus sunlight to a point, generating temperatures exceeding 3000°C for solar thermal power
Weather balloons expand as they rise because atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, and Boyle's law predicts volume increase at lower pressure
Rutherford's experiment was done with gold because it's malleable enough to make foils only a few atoms thick—thicker foils would scatter too many alpha particles
Airplane wing lift: the curved upper surface forces air to move faster (lower pressure above) than the flat lower surface (higher pressure below), creating net upward force via Bernoulli's principle

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reflection and Mirrors.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Reflection and Mirrors?

options.text: Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots
Musical instrument design: the length and shape of brass instruments determine which harmonics are produced; a bugle plays only natural harmonics of its tube length
Concert hall acoustics: architects design room dimensions, wall shapes, and absorption materials to optimize reverberation time (1.5-2.5 seconds ideal for orchestras)
Blood pressure measurement: a sphygmomanometer measures arterial pressure as the heart pumps—systolic (heart contracting) and diastolic (heart relaxing) values in mmHg

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reflection and Mirrors.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Reflection and Mirrors in practice?

options.text: Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy
Anti-reflective coatings on lenses use thin-film destructive interference: film thickness is chosen so reflected waves cancel, maximizing transmitted light
Telescopes use concave mirrors to collect and focus starlight: the Hubble Space Telescope has a 2.4-meter primary concave mirror
Atomic clocks use the precise frequency of electron transitions in cesium atoms (9.19 GHz) to define the second—accurate to 1 part in 10¹⁴

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reflection and Mirrors.

summary_sections.title: Understanding Refraction and Snell's Law
summary_sections.content_html: Refraction and Snell's Law is an important topic in AP Physics 2 covering fundamental concepts and principles.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts and Vocabulary
summary_sections.content_html: Key concepts in Refraction and Snell's Law include terminology, processes, and relationships specific to this topic.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications and Examples

summary_sections.content_html: Understanding Refraction and Snell's Law requires grasping how its concepts interact and apply to specific situations.

flashcards.question: Define: Thermodynamics
flashcards.answer: The study of heat, energy, and the relationships between thermal, mechanical, and other forms of energy

flashcards.question: Define: Entropy
flashcards.answer: A measure of disorder in a system; the second law states that entropy in an isolated system always increases

flashcards.question: Define: Electric Field
flashcards.answer: A region of space around a charged object where another charged object experiences a force

flashcards.question: Define: Magnetic Field
flashcards.answer: A region of space around a magnet or moving charge where magnetic forces are exerted

flashcards.question: Define: Electromagnetic Wave
flashcards.answer: A wave that consists of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation

flashcards.question: Define: Capacitor
flashcards.answer: A device that stores electrical energy by accumulating electric charge on two conducting plates

flashcards.question: Define: Resistance
flashcards.answer: The opposition to the flow of electric current through a material, measured in ohms

flashcards.question: Define: Interference
flashcards.answer: The phenomenon where two or more waves overlap and combine to create a resultant wave

flashcards.question: Which of the following is the primary way water returns to the atmosphere?
flashcards.answer: Evaporation from oceans and transpiration from plants

flashcards.question: Which human activity most directly affects the water cycle?
flashcards.answer: Urbanization reduces infiltration and increases runoff

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Index of refraction"?

options.text: 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + ...; total voltage = sum of individual voltages; same charge on each capacitor
Intensity I ∝ A²; for spherical waves: I = P/(4πr²), intensity decreases as 1/r²
J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron
n = c/v; the ratio of light speed in vacuum to light speed in the medium; n > 1 for all materials (n = 1.0 for vacuum, 1.33 for water, 1.5 for glass)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Index of refraction" is defined as: n = c/v; the ratio of light speed in vacuum to light speed in the medium; n > 1 for all materials (n = 1.0 for vacuum, 1.33 for water, 1.5 for glass). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Snell's law"?

options.text: Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects
d_o > 0 (real object); d_i > 0 (real image, opposite side); d_i  0 (converging); f < 0 (diverging)
Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit)
n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; relates angles of incidence and refraction to the indices of refraction of two media

quiz_questions.explanation: "Snell's law" is defined as: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; relates angles of incidence and refraction to the indices of refraction of two media. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Total internal reflection"?

options.text: Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted
When two waves meet, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference patterns
V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt); alternates between +V₀ and -V₀; ω = 2πf; US standard: 120V RMS at 60 Hz
F = kq₁q₂/r²; k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²; inversely proportional to r²; analogous to gravitational force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Total internal reflection" is defined as: Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Dispersion"?

options.text: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows
τ = nIAB sin θ; n = number of turns, I = current, A = loop area, B = field strength, θ = angle between area vector and B
Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly
F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Dispersion" is defined as: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Apparent depth"?

options.text: Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe
Strong attraction and permanent magnetization; magnetic domains align cooperatively; responsible for permanent magnets
B = μ₀I/(2πr); circular field lines around a long straight wire; strength decreases with distance r from wire

quiz_questions.explanation: "Apparent depth" is defined as: Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "n = c/v; the ratio of light speed in vacuum to light speed in the medium; n > 1 for all materials (n = 1.0 for vacuum, 1.33 for water, 1.5 for glass)"?

options.text: Wave energy
Electric field
Index of refraction
Root-mean-square speed

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Index of refraction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; relates angles of incidence and refraction to the indices of refraction of two media"?

options.text: Bernoulli's equation
Ohm's law
Snell's law
Convex mirror

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Snell's law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted"?

options.text: Total internal reflection
Magnetic field of a wire
Sound intensity level
Absorption spectrum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Total internal reflection".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows"?

options.text: Right-hand rule (force)
Dispersion
Brewster's angle
Convex mirror

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Dispersion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)"?

options.text: Pauli exclusion principle
Apparent depth
Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Root-mean-square speed

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Apparent depth".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Refraction and Snell's Law is accurate?

options.text: Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics governs the direction of natural processes.
Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.
Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).
Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Magnetic Properties of Materials classifies materials by their response to external magnetic fields.
Snell's law: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂, where n is the index of refraction (n = c/v).
EMF can be induced by changing B, changing A, or changing the angle θ between B and the area vector.
The law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection (θᵢ = θᵣ), measured from the normal.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (higher n) and away when entering a less dense medium.
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.
In hydrogen: E_n = -13.6 eV/n².
Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Plane mirrors produce virtual, upright, same-size images located behind the mirror.
Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).
Total internal reflection occurs when light hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle: θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁), and only when going from high-n to low-n medium.
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Electromagnetic waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.
Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.
A parallel-plate capacitor has capacitance C = ε₀A/d (area A, plate separation d).
The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts
Fiber optics: light pulses travel through thin glass fibers via total internal reflection, carrying internet data at near-light speed over thousands of kilometers

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone
Flash memory (USB drives, SSDs) stores data using quantum tunneling: electrons tunnel through a thin oxide layer to charge a floating gate, representing binary data
Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Refraction and Snell's Law applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Atomic clocks use the precise frequency of electron transitions in cesium atoms (9.19 GHz) to define the second—accurate to 1 part in 10¹⁴
Eyeglasses and contact lenses correct vision by refracting light to focus images correctly on the retina, compensating for the eye's refractive errors
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Fiber optics: light pulses travel through thin glass fibers via total internal reflection, carrying internet data at near-light speed over thousands of kilometers
Defibrillators deliver a large potential difference (~1000-3000 V) across the heart to reset its electrical rhythm during cardiac arrest
Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy
Scanning tunneling microscopes use quantum tunneling current between a sharp tip and a surface to image individual atoms on surfaces with sub-atomic resolution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Refraction and Snell's Law in practice?

options.text: Phone chargers contain transformers (and rectifiers) that convert 120V AC to 5-20V DC for safe device charging
Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles
Radio telescope arrays (like the VLA) combine signals from many dishes using interference principles to achieve resolution equivalent to a single dish spanning kilometers
Arrow of time: entropy increase explains why ice melts in warm rooms but warm rooms don't spontaneously freeze—the second law governs the direction of all natural processes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Converging lens (convex)"?

options.text: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) in decibels (dB); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing; each 10 dB = 10× intensity
Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT
f > 0; brings parallel light to a real focal point; produces real or virtual images depending on object position
P_total = P₁ + P₂; equivalent to adding the reciprocals of focal lengths

quiz_questions.explanation: "Converging lens (convex)" is defined as: f > 0; brings parallel light to a real focal point; produces real or virtual images depending on object position. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Diverging lens (concave)"?

options.text: Diagnostic imaging (gamma cameras, PET) and therapy (targeted radiation, brachytherapy) using radioactive isotopes
Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern
ᴬ_Z X: A = mass number (p + n), Z = atomic number (protons), N = A - Z = neutrons; isotopes differ in N
f < 0; causes parallel light to diverge as if from a virtual focal point; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images

quiz_questions.explanation: "Diverging lens (concave)" is defined as: f < 0; causes parallel light to diverge as if from a virtual focal point; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Thin lens equation"?

options.text: 1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂); relates focal length to the lens material (n) and surface curvatures (R₁, R₂)
Bright colored lines on a dark background; produced by excited atoms emitting photons at characteristic frequencies
J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron
1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; same form as mirror equation; positive d_i = real image (opposite side); negative d_i = virtual image (same side)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Thin lens equation" is defined as: 1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; same form as mirror equation; positive d_i = real image (opposite side); negative d_i = virtual image (same side). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ray diagrams"?

options.text: Three principal rays: (1) parallel to axis → through F, (2) through center → straight, (3) through F → parallel to axis
Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)
1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂); relates focal length to the lens material (n) and surface curvatures (R₁, R₂)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ray diagrams" is defined as: Three principal rays: (1) parallel to axis → through F, (2) through center → straight, (3) through F → parallel to axis. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Lenses, which best defines "Magnification"?

options.text: m = -d_i/d_o; same as for mirrors; determines image size and orientation
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)
ε = BLv; induced when a conductor of length L moves with velocity v perpendicular to field B
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier

quiz_questions.explanation: In Lenses, "Magnification" refers to: m = -d_i/d_o; same as for mirrors; determines image size and orientation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f > 0; brings parallel light to a real focal point; produces real or virtual images depending on object position"?

options.text: E-V relationship
Heat engine efficiency
Converging lens (convex)
Photon

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Converging lens (convex)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f < 0; causes parallel light to diverge as if from a virtual focal point; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images"?

options.text: Thomson's experiment
Diverging lens (concave)
Malus's law
Magnetic flux

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Diverging lens (concave)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; same form as mirror equation; positive d_i = real image (opposite side); negative d_i = virtual image (same side)"?

options.text: Self-inductance
Thin lens equation
Quantum tunneling
Longitudinal wave

Lenses use refraction to form images.

Radioactive decay follows N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), with half-life t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ.
Converging (convex) lenses are thicker in the middle and bring parallel rays to a focus; diverging (concave) lenses are thinner in the middle and spread rays apart.
Capacitors and Dielectrics store electrical energy in an electric field.

Applications of Quantum Ideas extends quantum principles to technologies and fundamental physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Lenses?

Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.
Key properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), and wave speed (v = fλ).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Lenses?

options.text: Cameras: converging lenses focus light from objects at varying distances onto the film/sensor by adjusting the lens-to-sensor distance (focusing)
Induction motors: AC current creates rotating magnetic fields that induce currents in the rotor, producing torque—powering most industrial machinery and household appliances
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules
Nuclear power plants: controlled fission of ²³⁵U in fuel rods heats water to produce steam that drives turbines, generating ~20% of US electricity without direct CO₂ emissions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Lenses?

options.text: Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots
Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules
Electric generators convert mechanical energy to electrical energy: rotating coils in magnetic fields produce alternating EMF (AC power) via Faraday's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Lenses applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Projectors: a converging lens creates a magnified real image of a small display on a distant screen, with the object placed just outside the focal length
Anti-reflective coatings on lenses use thin-film destructive interference: film thickness is chosen so reflected waves cancel, maximizing transmitted light
Noise barriers on highways: effective at blocking high-frequency (short λ) sound but less effective for low-frequency (long λ) sound that diffracts over the top
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Lenses?

options.text: CD/DVD reading: a laser reflects off pits and lands with different path lengths, creating constructive and destructive interference that encodes digital data
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts
Cameras: converging lenses focus light from objects at varying distances onto the film/sensor by adjusting the lens-to-sensor distance (focusing)
Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Lenses in practice?

options.text: Household wiring: outlets are wired in parallel (each at 120V) with circuit breakers in series (trip at 15-20A) to prevent overheating and fires
The electrical grid: power plants generate at ~20 kV, transformers step up to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission, then step down to 120/240V for homes—reducing losses by factors of thousands
Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.
